title: Why does .container use --bs-gutter-width for padding?
body:
The .container has this rule: ```css .container, .container-fluid, .container-lg, .container-md, .container-sm, .container-xl, .container-xxl {​​​​​​​​​​​​​​ padding-left: var(--bs-gutter-x,.75rem); padding-right: var(--bs-gutter-x,.75rem); ``` However, typically at this point, the `--bs-gutter-width` variable is not defined, so it will fall back to `.75rem`. Then in .row we have this: ```css .row { // This value depends on a SASS variable, I think. The front-end people set this to 1.5rem for a project I work on.   --bs-gutter-x: 1.5rem;   margin-left: calc(var(--bs-gutter-x)*-.5);   margin-right: calc(var(--bs-gutter-x)*-.5); ``` This is meant to compensate the padding on columns: ```css .row > * {     padding-left: calc(var(--bs-gutter-x)*.5);     padding-right: calc(var(--bs-gutter-x)*.5); ``` If you now have a .container within a .row, e.g. for a nested full-width section, the padding will be off: https://codepen.io/donqi/pen/BamWyRZ ```html

Starting the container

Starting a column

Container inside a full-width banner

``` So: - If .container is outside any .row, then using the `--bs-gutter-x` variable has no effect. - If .container is inside full-width section inside a .row, then using the `--bs-gutter-x` variable will cause mismatch with outer containers. - If a .container is used in any other place, that's probably wrong :)

title: Bootstrap, direct DOM manipulation & Vue's virtual DOM #310
body:
Hi there, 

This topic is w.r.t using Bootstrap (not just CSS, but JS components) in general with virtual-dom based frameworks like Vue & React. 

I would like to ask a very fundamental question, surprisingly I haven't really seen this discussed anywhere online expect for this stackoverflow [comment](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65547199/using-bootstrap-5-with-vue-3#comment125680515_66386443)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/73772701/157210617-f40dae7e-d044-4769-923b-743e88fec64e.png)

I know mixing Vue (virtual dom) + Jquery/Vanilla JS (that does direct dom manipulator) is an [anti-pattern](https://forum.vuejs.org/t/how-vue-2-0-work-with-jquery/3591/3), [anti-pattern](https://forum.vuejs.org/t/when-is-it-ok-to-directly-manipulate-the-dom/45818/3). 

Can anyone advise how you are using bootstrap JS components with Vue/React. If not, can we assume & establish that only the CSS part of Bootstrap is safe to use with Vue/React? 

I was also wondering about wrapper libraries like https://github.com/bootstrap-vue/bootstrap-vue/discussions/6931 & [bootstrap-vue-3](https://github.com/cdmoro/bootstrap-vue-3/discussions/310), if by design, they got some smarts to avoid direct dom manipulation by bootstrap.min.js. I have posted this same topic in their respective repos, but thought I will ask the question for any users here familiar with these wrapper libraries. 

Sorry, if it turns out to be a silly question, it's quite possible I might have misunderstood somethings! Thanks in advance.

title: What's reason to mass replacing SCSS variables to CSS variables?
body:
I'm seeing a massive replacement of SCSS with CSS variables in components. What caused these changes?

CSS variables doesn't look that impressive in **rendered** CSS.

pros:
- dynamic assign in CSS file
- inline assing in HTML (i.e. bad practice as this called in HTML manuals)

cons:
- slow render, uses more memory/CPU
- harder to debug/support
- less readable SCSS
author: WinterSilence

title: Breaking changes in the "main" branch by using CSS variables?
body:
Let's say I'm using bootstrap@5.1.3 and there is a piece of code like this:
```
@import "~bootstrap/scss/bootstrap";

.alert.alert-warning {
  border-width: $alert-border-radius * 2;  
}
```
It works on 5.1.3 because `$alert-border-radius` is a SCSS variable: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/1a6fdfae6be09b09eaced8f0e442ca6f7680a61e/scss/_variables.scss#L1451

Then when switching to the `main` branch the piece of code above is going to fail with error:

```
SassError: Undefined operation "var(--bs-border-radius) * 2"
```
That is because the same variable now contains a CSS variable value: https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/b165f35f7808b59f4e09ec518caa441e30830fbb/scss/_variables.scss#L1432
----
To mitigate this I'd need to update my code to use `calc`:
`border-width: calc(#{$alert-border-radius} * 2);`  

The question is: is it expected? I understand that this is just a `main` branch and not something which has been released. But I'd like to understand how it's going to be in the future. Will migration be required?

title: replace popper with floating ui to enable complete module bundling
body:
Popper is deprecated. The new version of popper is called FloatingUi. Bootstrap could replace Popper with [FloatingUI](https://github.com/floating-ui/floating-ui) (from the popper maintainers) and eliminate the last barriers (?) to use bootstrap as a module. 

[Taken from the docs](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.1/getting-started/javascript/#using-bootstrap-as-a-module): 
> Due to browser limitations, some of our plugins, namely Dropdown, Tooltip and Popover plugins, cannot be used in a <script> tag with module type because they depend on Popper.

### Why?

Module support, smaller bundle size, and last but not least, using popper in module frameworks like Svelte [leads to issues](https://github.com/svelte-add/svelte-add/issues/31#issuecomment-985880894). 

### Additional information
- https://github.com/shoelace-style/shoelace/discussions/601 just migrated from Popper to Floating UI too.

title: Add additional color utility classes similar to tailwind
body:
# Introduction I recently began using Tailwind in a new project and have been very pleased thus far. Yet, overall, I can see that I am losing a lot of speed and convenience compared to the bootstrap. One thing I noticed that I often felt missing since Bootstrap 2 was a way to customize the colors easily. I often felt constrained by the choice of only a few color groups (white, dark, success, primary, warning, and error), yet I didn't want to overwrite them. I wish I could color certain components easily while adhering to some loose form of color schema. Tailwind solved this very nicely with their utility classes. # Examples ```ruby bg-gray-50 # background in gray intensity 50, light gray bg-blue-100 # background in blue intensity 100, less light blue bg-sky-900 # background in "sky" intensity 900, pretty dark blue. border-blue-100 border-sky-200 text-fuchsia-400 dark:text-red-500 # set the text color to red in darkmode ``` Even further, these colors can be extended with a nice set of state modifiers: ```ruby hover:bg-red-500 focus:border-green-200 active:text-purple-800 dark:hover:text-white # set the text color to white in dark mode on hover ``` These can also be applied to certain borders only: ```ruby border-l-sky-300 # border left in color sky intensity 300 border-r-sky-300 # border right in color sky intensity 300 border-x-sky-300 # border right,left in color sky intensity 300 border-y-sky-300 # border top,bottom in color sky intensity 300 ``` ## A theoretical button example This enables one to create a custom button quite easily while being somewhat harmonic in terms of colors. ```html
What a nice custom violet button
``` # Simplicity Personally, I do not like the large numbers used and I see no reason why it shouldn't be something like "text-blue-5" instead of "text-blue-500". ```html
What a nice custom violet button
``` ### Why don't you write css instead? That is certainly possible, but I would most likely be forced to select a new set of colors each time. I do like the large but limited variety of colors given by Tailwind.  # Conclusion I am not a big fan of writing Tailwind since the difference between plain CSS and Tailwind is too minimal. Also, in Tailwind, I am forced to fix many browser quirks that are already solved with bootstrap. Yet, these color classes provided me with a very nice value-add compared to plain CSS. Therefore, I think they might be the right thing to be included in bootstrap as a new feature. There is no reason to remove the current color classes either; they could live side-by-side. `text-success` and `text-green-700` # Tailwind documentation https://tailwindcss.com/docs/background-color https://tailwindcss.com/docs/border-color https://tailwindcss.com/docs/text-color
author: stillhart-at-bechterew

title: Bootstrap Icons <i> tag versus <span>
body:
It is not semantically suggested to use `<i>` tag for an icon, for the following reason:

>The `<strong>`/`<b>` and `<em>`/`<i>` tags have exactly the same effect in most web browsers, but there is a fundamental difference between them: `<strong>` and `<em>` have a semantic meaning whereas `<b>` and `<i>` only convey styling information like CSS.
>
>While `<b>` can have simply no effect on a some devices with limited display or when a screen reader software is used by a blind person, `<strong>` will:
>
>Display the text bold in normal browsers
>Speak with lower tone when using a screen reader such as Jaws
>Consequently:
>
>in order to convey semantics, the `<b>` and `<i>` tags shall never be used,
>in order to convey styling information, the `<b>` and `<i>` should be avoided and CSS should be used instead. 

Is there any reasoning behind providing Bootstrap Icons on https://icons.getbootstrap.com/icons/ with an `<i>` tag instead of for example a `<span>`?

Yes, it is perfectly possible to do that when using the Icons in HTML, but it would be easier to just copy it with the proper HTML entity instead of having to change it each time, and I also think it would make the web a better place if the "big" ones (Bootstrap in this case) would just show the examples with the right tags by default.

Please apologise if I somehow am wrong with above thinking approach.

title: How to use Bootstrap 5 with Vite?
body:
How to use Bootstrap 5 with Vite? If you don't know what Vite is:

> Before ES modules were available in browsers, developers had no native mechanism for authoring JavaScript in a modularized fashion. This is why we are all familiar with the concept of "bundling": using tools that crawl, process and concatenate our source modules into files that can run in the browser.
> 
> Over time we have seen tools like [webpack](https://webpack.js.org/), [Rollup](https://rollupjs.org/) and [Parcel](https://parceljs.org/), which greatly improved the development experience for frontend developers.
> 
> However, as we start to build more and more ambitious applications, the amount of JavaScript we are dealing with also increased exponentially. It is not uncommon for large scale projects to contain thousands of modules. We are starting to hit a performance bottleneck for JavaScript based tooling: it can often take an unreasonably long wait (sometimes up to minutes!) to spin up a dev server, and even with HMR, file edits can take a couple seconds to be reflected in the browser. The slow feedback loop can greatly affect developers' productivity and happiness.
> 
> Vite aims to address these issues by leveraging new advancements in the ecosystem: the availability of native ES modules in the browser, and the rise of JavaScript tools written in compile-to-native languages.
-- https://vitejs.dev/guide/why.html

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6342102/161825624-19d87191-ae96-44e1-a955-864929a67652.png)


I've used Vite with Tailwind and it is FAST. Is it possible to get some documentation on how to use Bootstrap 5 with Vite? Thank you

title: React: autoprefixer: Replace color-adjust to print-color-adjust.
body:
Hi I try to make my first React app by some youtube tutorial. I have troubles with bootstrap. I install it, import it in the same way like tutorial do, but there  is the error autoprefixer: Replace color-adjust to print-color-adjust.. I found  here a  nice solution by @vineetdigit , but  Im pure  beginner and  I dont  understand what  author means with: "\rm -rf node_modules" and "restart the react app with "react-scripts start" " Thank you for your advice and your leniency.

This most certainly didn't work for me.

`npm install autoprefixer@10.4.5 --save-exact`

But following helped remove the warning.

In the package.json of the top level directory of the react app, add the following section after the dependencies: {..}

`"overrides":{
    "autoprefixer": "10.4.5"
  }`
delete package-lock.json
\rm -rf node_modules
npm install
restart the react app with "react-scripts start"
The warning is no more.

title: How to override component-level CSS variables?
body:
I’ve just installed the 5.2 beta as excited to try out CSS custom properties (variables)! I seem to be misunderstanding how they work, though.

In cards, I can see there are properties like `--bs-box-shadow`. So, I added a `<style>` tag with the following rule:

```html
<style>
body {
    --bs-card-box-shadow: 0 5px 10px black;
}
</style>
```

This doesn’t seem to have any effect, though. If I inspect a card on my page, it doesn’t seem to be picking up my defined value and instead has a “blank” value:

![Screenshot 2022-05-16 at 15 53 46](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/167312/168621764-b94fdbd1-97ed-48e0-a36b-a8f683cc0772.png)

![Screenshot 2022-05-16 at 15 53 34](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/167312/168621613-b289ee5b-7a6b-4568-8221-46f317cfc370.png)

It _does_ work if I define the value in a `.card` selector:

```html
<style>
.card {
    --bs-card-box-shadow: 0 5px 10px black;
}
</style>
```

But this to me defeats the point of having variables if we then have to define component-specific variables in many selectors?

I’m asking as I’m trying to introduce theming to an application and I’d like to just be able to set a load of custom properties on the `body` element (overriding Bootstrap’s set in `:root`), but this isn’t going to be feasible if I have to define property values in various CSS class selectors.

Why do property values set in a selector like `body` not propagate down to the components? Why do I have to define property values in component-specific selectors like `.card`?

title: Why not "defer" bootstrap javascript in <head>?
body:
With "async" and "defer" options for controlling when javascript is loaded, why does Boostrap documentation still recommend placing the bootstrap <script> tag  before the closing body tag </body> (https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.2/getting-started/introduction/)? It seems like antiquated advice. The "defer" attribute tells the browser to execute the javascript after the document is loaded and parsed (they wait if needed), right before DOMContentLoaded. According to (https://caniuse.com/script-defer), 97% of all browsers support "defer".  

As stated in this thread (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56105230/bootstrap-4-scripts-best-location-and-scripts-tags), "Head is for external resources. To have all dependencies out of the way at the start of the document is best practice. The `<head>` was made for exactly that. As the DOM is not loaded at that moment in page loading, this will fail all references to the DOM made there. There used to be a (dark) time, before defer, where it was common to get the advice to place the script-includes after `</body>` but before `</html>`."

This:
```
<head>
...
<script defer src="assets/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>
</head>
```

Instead of this:
```
...
<script src="assets/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

title: How was the defaults of $container-max-widths decided?
body:
I am splitting the breakpoint of xs into an additional smaller class, xxs, via a custom.scss file:

```
$grid-breakpoints: (
    xxs: 0,
    xs: 320px,
    sm: 576px,
    md: 768px,
    lg: 992px,
    xl: 1200px,
    xxl: 1400px
);
```
I also want to add a corresponding xs size to $container-max-widths. I would like to know the rationale behind the default max-widths (see below) so I can apply it to my custom xs size. What would you recommend? 

Default _variables.scss file:
```
$container-max-widths: (
  sm: 540px,
  md: 720px,
  lg: 960px,
  xl: 1140px,
  xxl: 1320px
) !default;
```
For now, I am approximating the size by multiplying the ratio of $container-max-widths to $grid-breakpoints of the sm size (540/576= 0.9375) with my xs value (320*0.9375=300) to come up with the following for my custom.scss:

```
$container-max-widths: (
  xs: 300px,
  sm: 540px,
  md: 720px,
  lg: 960px,
  xl: 1140px,
  xxl: 1320px
)
```
Does this seem logical?

title: Using CSS Grid with Bootstrap? Is it time, yet?
body:
I've been watching a few videos on the wonders of the newer CSS Grid layout module available in modern CSS. Boostrap, since v5.1.0, offers an opt-in experimental CSS Grid system. While I would like to start using CSS Grid in my designs, I am hesitant because mobile visitors have to use the latest versions of their browser for it to work. For example, https://caniuse.com/css-grid lists Firefox 101 (for android) as the earliest version that supports CSS Grid, and it was just released in May of this year. Unless I am willing to provide a fallback for just-out-of-date browsers, it still seems premature to use CSS grid?

Are you using CSS Grid yet in your responsive website designs for mobile devices? Or, is it still Flexbox all the way?

title: [Brainstorming]: Optimization - Get rid of unused css.
body:
Let's all agree that having unused code in production is terrible, no one likes it, and it is a waste of resources; It will affect the loading time of a web page since the browser is required to fetch more data than needed. Therefore, there should be a way to pull only the necessary data upon request. However, implementing such a mechanism is a bit challenging, hence posting this in a discussion. 

Right off the bat, I can think of having an api that handles requests and returns only the css elements needed for consumption. For instance, say we have the following endpoint: `https://bootstrap.com/css/?elements=`, a user can pull specific css elements like btn, btn-primary, mg-3, p-5, and the request becomes:

`https://bootstrap.com/css/?elements=btn,btn-primary,mg-3,p-5`

Another solution I can think of is tearing down the huge `bootstrap.min.css` file into multiple small files, say one file called `common.css`, which contains definitions for common css elements like `:root`, `@media` queries, and things of that nature. This way, a user can pull specific css files stored on edge, cdn... For example, if a user wants to use `btn`, then they have to specify the name of the file in the head tag like the following:

```html
<link
  rel="stylesheet"
  href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.1.3/dist/css/common.css"
/>
<link
  rel="stylesheet"
  href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.1.3/dist/css/btn.css"
/>
```

Another solution for this is to manually download and store the `bootstrap.min.css` file and adjust its content accordingly. However, doing such a task is hell since it requires scanning around 10k lines of css. I know that there are tools to automate such tasks. However, such tools will remove media queries and such; take the following as an example: 

![Screenshot from 2022-07-21 08-54-19](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/62179149/180146571-9dc1f206-b849-4b4d-8637-782461be965e.png)

Chrome coverage tool reported it as unused css. Therefore, it is not helpful to use such tools cause of false positives.

That is all i can think of at the moment. What do you think about such a problem?

title: Utilities to warkaround calc() limits
body:
Hi folks,

There are lots of questions on Stack Overflow, how to do something in pure CSS that can't be achieved due to `calc()` limits.

To give you just one example, https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38617025/using-n-inside-calc (Duplicates: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/28002684/is-there-a-way-to-use-the-n-value-in-nth-childn, https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42434460/index-of-nth-child-within-the-css-rule and so on, hundreds of votes in total).

What if create utility classes to workaround this? For instance, after adding `indexed` class to an element you would get a variable `--item-index` (or similar named) for each child of the element, that could be used in `calc()`. Implementation is trivial, but it seems to be in demand.

Another example: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/8720931/can-css-detect-the-number-of-children-an-element-has (383 votes). After adding a class, say, `count-children`, BS could set a variable `--children-number` to use within `calc()`.

Sorry, if the idea doesn't belong to Bootstrap domain.

Regards,

title: Inconsistent behavior of Scrollspy after 5.2 update
body:
Dear all,

tl;dr: Scrollspy in 5.2 highlights different items depending on scroll direction, in particular items above the screen when scrolling up.

I'm currently updating a project from 5.1.3 to 5.2.0 and I can't get Scrollspy to work in the same way. I have a page which has two DIVs next to each other. The left-hand side DIV contains the NAV menu (table of contents), the right-hand side DIV contains an HTML document. The HTML document (pulled from a database) can become quite lengthy with many sections, so the table of contents may become so long that both DIVs are scrollable. Consequently, the "spy" is not on the body, but on the DIV with the actual content.

With 5.1.3, the two DIVs were set up as:
`<nav id="navbar-minutes" class="navbar navbar-light>`

And:
`<div class="containter-meeting-minutes" data-bs-spy="scroll" data-bs-target="#navbar-minutes" data-bs-offset="160">`

Respectively. This worked consistently with 5.1.3 (at least in Chrome and Safari) that when scrolling either way, whatever section was at the top of the right-hand side DIV, was highlighted in the NAV menu on the left. Also, when clicking on a link in the NAV menu, the right-hand side scrolled to the right position, and again the corresponding on the left was highlighted correctly.

With 5.2.0, I have changed the the second DIVs definition to:
`<div class="containter-meeting-minutes" data-bs-spy="scroll" data-bs-target="#navbar-minutes" data-bs-root-margin="160px 0px 0px 0px">`

But the behavior is very different:
- When scrolling down, whatever section just enters at the bottom of the DIV gets highlighted in the table of contents, instead of when it reaches the top. So when I click on a section, and it is a shorter section (more than once section visible on the screen), it may happen that the highlighting in the NAV menu is one or two items below from where I clicked.
- When scrolling up, the section that is highlighted is the one that just enters the top of the screen, even before it enters the top of the DIV.
- When scrolling up and down alternately in an area of the document where there are three short sections visible on the screen, the highlighting in the table of contents jumps up and down by 4-5 menu entries at once, because it jumps from the section at the bottom of the DIV when scrolling down to the section at (above) the top of the DIV when scrolling up, and vice versa.

Through trial and error, I have now set it to:
`<div class="containter-meeting-minutes" data-bs-spy="scroll" data-bs-target="#navbar-minutes" data-bs-root-margin="160px 0px -90% 0px">`

This probably breaks the system since I've just moved the bottom margin to very close to the top of my DIV. But at least this gives me very close to the intended behavior when scrolling down, either by scrolling or by clicking on a table of contents entry further down. The section that is highlighted by scrollspy now consistently is the one at or near the top of the right-hand side DIV. WooHoo!

But the highlighting still is all over the place when scrolling up. The section that gets highlighted is always above the visible area. 

It feels like the margin isn't applied when scrolling up, only when scrolling down.

Example:

After scrolling down, the section at the top of the screen is correctly highlighted:
![scrolling_down](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66065605/184544685-26a061ff-c379-43c7-8e3d-d38f3ca6d290.png)

After scrolling up (here by clicking on the menu item highlighted in grey), the section highlighted by scrollspy is two items above. The corresponding section is well above the visible area.
![scrolling_up](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66065605/184544715-782bf380-be59-4c79-b65e-4b6e4086d4de.png)

Note: the DIV the scrollspy is on is the white area on the right. The 160px offset/top margin accommodates the fixed headings. 

Maybe I'm doing it wrong. Maybe I'm using it for something it's not intended for. In any case, I'd appreciate any ideas. Thanks!

author: terabytesoftw

title: Activatable button
body:
Greetings,

I would like to propose an addition to the button groups that we could for now refer to as an **activatable button**. 

The idea of an activatable button is inspired by emergency buttons which have a protective cover like this:
https://www.securitysafetyproducts.co.uk/images/upload/products_image5-11545-d.jpg

The button is off by default but cannot be accidentally clicked until activated. I have made a rough example here:
https://stackblitz.com/edit/3tkzv2-8fjkd4?file=index.html

The advantages of working with an activatable button are:
- no need for confirmation modals, whose action can be anywhere on the page (violation of Fitt's law);
- safety: no fear of accidentally triggering whatever action is behind the button as it is off by default;

It may be improved by using a switch instead of a checkbox or simply a checkbox to activate.

I don't think I've seen this before but I'm happy to be proved wrong.

title: Why are breakpoints the building blocks of responsive design?
body:
Bootstrap's documentation states that breakpoints are the building blocks of responsive design. However, I don't quite understand why this is so. For example, I have a navbar that I want to 'shrinkwrap' to the inherent width of the content, and break into a burger menu only when the viewport gets too narrow to display the navbar at that width: ![navbar](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/793830/190827375-c3ef5b8f-353a-4a7b-97db-6b74d74bbb61.png) My understanding is that I can't really achieve this with Bootstrap, and that it's not designed to let me do it. The navbar is supposed to collapse at a higher width that its 'shrinkwrapped' width, when there is still some black space to the right of it. Why is this a better way to do responsive design? To underscore what I'm talking about, look at the following navbar. It's styled like this: ```
  • Home
    • [...]
``` Because of `d-inline-flex`, it shrinkwraps so the background nearly fits the content, which is desired. But Bootstrap either makes me break at a wider width than the navbar, like 992px with `.navbar-expand-lg`: ![navbar1](https://i.stack.imgur.com/xOdZl.gif) ... or not break at all, displaying a horizontal scrollbar instead of breaking just when the viewport gets too small: ![navbar2](https://i.stack.imgur.com/GeBkK.gif) Neither of these are desirable to me. Why doesn't Bootstrap cater for breaking to a burger menu at the natural content width?

title: :focus-visible not supported by all old browsers
body:
Hello all,

I am currently working on focus for Boosted and I have a problem with the PR "Rework button focus/active styling, with extra changes for checks/radios #37026".

@patrickhlauke switched from ':focus' to ':focus-visible' arguing that ':focus-visible' is now supported by most browsers.

According to [https://caniuse.com/?search=focus-visible](https://caniuse.com/?search=focus-visible) it is mostly true.

But for Firefox 4-84, it is mentioned that it needs the prefix -moz-focusring.
According to [https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/:-moz-focusring](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/:-moz-focusring ), this prefix is non-standard and is not on a standards track so we should not use it on production.
In addition, it may not work even after adding the prefix -moz-focusring as I tested on Browserstack.

For Chrome, it seems not supported by default too.

Howewer if we refer to [.browserslistrc file](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v5.2.2/.browserslistrc) Bootstrap is supposed to support old browsers (e.g. Firefox 68, etc.).

So what to do here?

Stop using ':focus-visible' since it's not supported by all old browsers?
Keep using it and add a fallback for some browsers like Firefox 60 to 84?
Keep using it and update the list of supported browsers on [.browserslistrc file](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v5.2.2/.browserslistrc)?

Best regards.
Mewen
